packs.name: Strict Git
packs.description: Stricter git protections: blocks all force pushes, rebases, and history rewriting operations

structural_patterns.name: push-force-any
structural_patterns.reason: Force push (even with --force-with-lease) can rewrite remote history. Disabled in strict mode.
structural_patterns.explanation: Force pushing rewrites remote branch history, which can cause collaborators to lose commits. Even --force-with-lease, which is safer, can overwrite work if not used carefully. In strict mode, all force pushes are blocked.

structural_patterns.name: rebase
structural_patterns.reason: git rebase rewrites commit history. Disabled in strict mode.
structural_patterns.explanation: Rebase rewrites commit history by replaying commits on top of another base. When done on shared branches, this causes diverged history for other contributors.

structural_patterns.name: commit-amend
structural_patterns.reason: git commit --amend rewrites the last commit. Disabled in strict mode.
structural_patterns.explanation: Amending a commit rewrites it with a new hash. If the original commit was already pushed to a shared branch, this creates a diverged history that requires a force push to reconcile.

structural_patterns.name: cherry-pick
structural_patterns.reason: git cherry-pick can introduce duplicate commits. Review carefully.
structural_patterns.explanation: Cherry-picking copies commits from one branch to another, creating duplicate commits with different hashes. This can complicate merges and create confusing history.

structural_patterns.name: filter-branch
structural_patterns.reason: git filter-branch rewrites entire repository history. Extremely dangerous!
structural_patterns.explanation: filter-branch rewrites every commit in the repository's history. All collaborators will have diverged history and must re-clone or do complex rebases. Once pushed, this is very difficult to recover from.

structural_patterns.name: filter-repo
structural_patterns.reason: git filter-repo rewrites repository history. Review carefully.
structural_patterns.explanation: filter-repo is the modern replacement for filter-branch and rewrites repository history. All collaborators will have diverged history after this operation.

structural_patterns.name: reflog-expire
structural_patterns.reason: git reflog expire removes reflog entries needed for recovery.
structural_patterns.explanation: The reflog is git's safety net that allows recovery of commits even after hard resets, branch deletions, or other destructive operations. Expiring reflog entries removes this recovery capability.

structural_patterns.name: gc-aggressive
structural_patterns.reason: git gc with aggressive/prune options can remove recoverable objects.
structural_patterns.explanation: Running git gc with --aggressive or --prune can permanently remove loose objects that could otherwise be used to recover lost commits.

structural_patterns.name: worktree-remove
structural_patterns.reason: git worktree remove deletes a linked working tree.
structural_patterns.explanation: Removing a worktree deletes the linked working directory. Any uncommitted changes in that worktree will be lost.

structural_patterns.name: submodule-deinit
structural_patterns.reason: git submodule deinit removes submodule configuration.
structural_patterns.explanation: deinit removes the submodule's working directory content and clears its configuration from .git/config. The submodule definition in .gitmodules remains but the local checkout is removed.

structural_patterns.name: push-master
structural_patterns.reason: Direct push to master is blocked. Use a Pull Request.
structural_patterns.explanation: Pushing directly to the master branch bypasses code review and CI checks. Use a feature branch and open a Pull Request to merge changes into master.
